DOGE SURVIVOR GUIDE / FIRST PARTNER CHALLENGE PACKET — navigating the federal bid process (GovCon EDU / Federal Help Center)

ACTION PLAN (step-by-step activities to focus and get results, sequenced across 30 / 90 / 180 / 360 days):
- Respond to RFI / Sources Sought.
- Update your website to include federal procurement.
- Identify bid opportunities that match your NAICS (find partners/suppliers needed for the bid).
- Set up vendor credit with suppliers; establish trade accounts.
- Set up a meeting with at least one agency to discuss market opportunities.
- Attend industry events related to your federal pipeline (govevents.com, APEX, SAM.gov).
- Get on the supplier bid list for top 25 federal suppliers.
- Research whether top sub-agencies have upcoming bid opportunities.
- Attend at least one federal site walkthrough/visit.
- Attend meetings with local agencies to discuss their needs.
- Identify task-order / IDIQ contracts for your NAICS (forecast, recompete).
- Apply for small business certification.
- Gather past-customer testimonials and referrals (corporate, SLED, etc.).
- Identify and bid on subcontract opportunities.
- Meet with buyers regarding upcoming contract vehicles and forecast lists.
- Submit bids for at least 10 contracts; evaluate results against winning competitors.
- Monitor contract awards and identify service opportunities.

BID / NO-BID DECISION FRAMEWORK — rate each criterion Positive (10/7) / Neutral (6/4/3) / Negative (2/0):
- Ability to respond (background experience and technical capability): Positive = understand the problem and can respond, can meet/exceed every requirement including past performance, strong in-house/technically superior. Neutral = average experience, can be acquired, technically capable. Negative = weak experience or new area, not technically qualified.
- Proposed team and personnel (subs are an option): Positive = best available, may enhance offering. Negative = second best, dilutes scope.
- Price strategy: Positive = we know the price-to-win, can provide an honest credible bid within a known price structure. Neutral = good idea of price-to-win, reasonable/competitive. Negative = guessing the price-to-win, must cut corners/margins.
- Customer knowledge and rapport: Positive = good working relationships, we know the customer's needs; sole source or customer prefers us. Neutral = we are known but relationships not cultivated. Negative = unknown to the customer, we don't know their needs.
- Competition: Positive = inside track, good write-up. Neutral = open/neutral customer and we know how to beat the competition. Negative = unknown competition, may be wired.
- Market intelligence: Positive = up-to-date. Negative = surprised by RFP issuance or requirements.
- Company resources for proposal development and contract execution: Positive = easily available to develop a winning proposal and execute. Negative = average/unavailable.
- Facilities: Positive = available and favorably located. Neutral = available / no facility required or acquired quickly.

TEAMING (First Partner Challenge): pick an industry (top service-business NAICS by sector — Technology/Software: 513210, 541519, 541512, 541511, 518210; Construction: 236220, 237990, 237310, 238220, 238210; Engineering/Consulting: 541330, 541715, 541611). Teaming pre-qualification checklist: clear communication, relevant experience. Day-by-day: build a call sheet, role-play, have an initial meeting with a company (set the goal for the call, what to say, what to look for, how to pitch your services), then form the partnership and agree next steps (charging money, signed contract).

SBA PROFILE: understand the SBA profile (DSBS) for funding/contracting success; avoid common mistakes; gather required documentation; follow best practices.

MARKET RESEARCH STEPS: identify your market (who your customers are and their needs); analyze competitors (strengths/weaknesses of key players); assess demand (market potential and customer interest). Leverage AI/"Govcon GPT" to enhance efficiency.
